question: Quais são as suas necessidades de suporte antecipadas da FFA e de nossos parceiros do programa, e qual é a sua visão para o envolvimento e colaboração contínuos com a FFA avançando?
answer: Acredito que 'gerar negócios' na prática é a melhor forma de apoiar startups como a nossa. question: Quais são as suas necessidades de suporte antecipadas da FFA e de nossos parceiros do programa, e qual é a sua visão para o envolvimento e colaboração contínuos com a FFA avançando?
answer: Nossa demanda por suporte visa ampliar o alcance da plataforma e fomentar o envolvimento das comunidades na colaboração com organizações públicas e privadas, visando impulsionar a gestão sustentável dos recursos hídricos. Estamos em busca de parcerias com organizações públicas e privadas que tenham o interesse em minimizar os riscos associados ao impacto negativo em comunidades, decorrente de seus processos produtivos. Além disso, buscamos parceiros que estejam comprometidos em gerar impacto positivo nas comunidades, tanto dentro quanto fora de sua região de atuação, com o objetivo de promover a gestão sustentável dos recursos hídricos. A nossa abordagem se baseia em uma plataforma colaborativa que integra a comunidade, organizações públicas e privadas. Esta integração visa criar um ambiente propício para a promoção efetiva da gestão sustentável dos recursos hídricos, incentivando a participação ativa de todas as partes interessadas. Permitindo assim, acompanhar os indicadores de interesse, promover decisões orientada por dados, além de, criar uma base de informações que poderá contribuir com a criação de politicas públicas e impacto positivo direto nas comunidades atendidas.

question: Quais são as suas necessidades de suporte antecipadas da FFA e de nossos parceiros do programa, e qual é a sua visão para o envolvimento e colaboração contínuos com a FFA avançando?
answer: Contribuir para a Comunidade: Participar ativamente na comunidade FFA, compartilhando nossas experiências, aprendizados e conhecimentos, contribuindo para o crescimento coletivo. Mentoria Recíproca: Se apropriado, oferecer mentorias para novas startups que ingressam no programa, contribuindo para a diversidade de perspectivas e aprimorando a colaboração entre os participantes. Engajamento em Programas Futuros: Manifestar interesse em futuros programas, caso se alinhem com nossas metas de crescimento e desenvolvimento. Participação em Eventos e Iniciativas: Estar envolvido em eventos, workshops ou iniciativas promovidas pela FFA que possam beneficiar a GreenWave Enterprises e reforçar nosso compromisso com a missão da FFA.

question: Can you share the top 5 key business and impact updates, changes or improvements that have taken place since the accelerator programme ended last year?
answer: 1. Thematic groups (Bootcamp 1 on Efficient Water and Sanitation Services), provided a clear focus on 4 impact areas: Environment (re-use waste, save water, reduce greenhouse gas emissions); Education (learning outcomes and school attendance); Economic (household income, reduced sanitation costs, water savings); and Health (reduce illness, waterborne diseases, and diarrhoea). EverGreen Access’s specific causal pathway was somewhat crudely unpacked in terms of Activities (SavvyLoo sales, logistics savings, lower PSP costs, and beneficial use of waste-streams), Outputs (interactions on communities as users, bio-degradable waste streams, faster servicing, savings in water and logistics), Intermediate Outcomes (knowledge and behaviour such as less pollution, better access and more use of SavvyLoos, economic potential of communities), and Outcomes (societal impact: water savings, reduced greenhouse gas emissions, better school attendance, waste reused, fewer deaths). In my view, the EverGreen Access causal pathway aligns well with the four Thematic Group’s impact areas. We have continued to use EverGreen Access’s specific causal pathway in our redesign SavvyLoo v4 and business thinking with both Academic Research Institute and Karen Fischer (industrial design). 2. My single biggest “mindset change” came from Chrispin Russell’s presentation on “ideation to Minimum Viable Product (MVP)” during November 2022, and a further conversation with him during the following week. Crispin had started with his “Ozone” ideation in 2009 (some 13 years ago). In parallel, EverGreen Access’s SavvyLoo started its product development with the Vaal University of Technology during 2013 and completed SavvyLoo v1 and v2 field trials from 2015 to 2018, which worked well as MVPs. EverGreen Access then moved its product development to Skeg based in Cape Town (1 400 kms away) in 2019, and Skeg manufactured SavvyLoo v3 for field trials during 2020/2021. Skeg’s SavvyLoo v3 models failed in three key respects: “Hot box” design failed; “Drying time” of solids was inadequate; “Actuator” was over designed (excessive movement which, in turn, reduced drying time). During his presentation and discussion, Chrispin stressed: (a) the importance of working with the right product development company (he had also previously removed product development from Skeg), (b) “start small”, de-risk, “walk first” and then “build up”, (c) continuously test assumptions. This interaction with Chrispin, completely changed the direction of the product development of SavvyLoo: (a) terminate with Skeg after the first 2 models were delivered and tested, (b) engage with Sandro of Academic Research Institute, who recommended a completing a Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) during 2022/2023 (completed), and (c) re-engage with Karen Fischer of the Vaal University of Technology on SavvyLoo v4 design, to incorporate changes/improvements from the Skeg prototypes and FMEA results (2024). This is currently in progress. 3. I use The Theory of Change model for EverGreen Access, flowing from the Thematic groups (Bootcamp 1), for all my stakeholder interactions, as a diagram. I prefer the simple earlier diagrammatic version (Excel) over the proposed formal one Sundus Aziz and I used to derive measures. Principles: • SavvyLoo as a tool (development currently in-progress) provides →: • Interim Outcomes: (a) “desiccated waste streams (i.e. no sludge leakage in surface or groundwater)”, (b) “lower logistics requirements (i.e. less risk of polluting through servicing)”, (c) “greater access and service envelopes (i.e. greater access to hygiene)”, and (d) “waste valorisation partners/licensees (i.e. shared economic value)”. These result in →: • Outputs: (a) “cleaner waterways, water supplies and environment”, (b) “less respiratory disease”, (c) “healthier communities and schools”, (d) “natural fertilisers, energy and urea”. These, in turn, mean →: • Ultimately: “Improve livelihoods, sustainably”. 4. Involvement of Legacy Impact Trust (UJ PEETS). UJ PEETS team conducted a technology audit study for the existing prototype on 5 December 2023, several teleconference meetings, and a meeting with the Head of UJ PEETS on 14 February 2024. UJ PEETS to make recommendations on the management of the urea (~1.5 l per person per day) and the dry faecal matter (~ 0.45 kg per person per day), taking into considerations the logistic implications for the removal of the material from the pilot sites to where they will be managed. Options include: Black Soldier Fly (UJ PEETS are already involved in another BSF project), conversion into organic waste (UJ PEETS already involved in similar projects), possible use in biogas (UJ PEETS already involved in similar projects), and proposed biomimicry to assist with the cleaning of the toilet system to eliminate or reduce dependency on water for cleaning; UJ PEETS will undertake testing of prototypes. We are jointly in the process of developing a scope of work for this long term project. 5. Stated focus on WASH, and aligned to the achievement of World Health Organization (WHO) and the United Nations (UN) stated Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), and most particularly contributing to achievement of: • Goal 3 (good health and well-being): that addresses all major health priorities and calls for (inter alia) ending communicable diseases, reducing non-communicable diseases and other health hazards. • Goal 6 (clean water and sanitation): that aims to tackle challenges related to drinking water, sanitation and hygiene for populations, as well as to water-related ecosystems. • Goal 11 (sustainable cities and communities): that addresses the enormous challenges associated with rapid urbanisation, including inadequate housing, increased air pollution, lack of access to basic services and infrastructure, and inadequate management of waste.

question: Current needs
answer: We are at a critical make-or-break juncture in our growth trajectory, and we believe our current needs overlap well with what we understand to be the intent of the Access Accelerator. We seek support to accelerate our growth to reach more customers to benefit health and hygiene. Aurelia’s business acumen would be invaluable as we think through marketing strategies, new routes to market, improving management reports including deeper embedding of our monitoring and reporting against impact metrics, streamlining operations, financial modeling for new products, and more while improving WASH and hygiene. Silverline Capital Social Business aligns to our commitment to ending poverty and addressing the climate crisis through social justice business activities. I would hope that this Accelerator can help us set out clear outcomes and key deliverables monthly and quarterly and help our various team members strengthen their own leadership and self-direction to meet these targets. We believe the new diaper products we seek to introduce are a perfect fit in particular for the Accelerator - both for product introduction and for the reproductive health, hygiene, and disease prevention support we can deliver with the Nia HealthLink for parents of young children. Our hybrid diapers will be disruptive to the market, offering the outer reusable diaper for nearly at-cost, while we introduce small sachets of the disposable inner diapers which are expected to be a fraction of the cost of traditional, disposable diapers, while being >95% biodegradable. It will take an extraordinary team to launch this product and we think it will be a very satisfying journey together with mentors and our team if we can do it right. We would welcome further support through technical assistance to model out the multi-faceted positive environmental impacts that come with locally manufacturing pads as well as locally manufacturing our own fluff pulp. In addition, as we consider continental expansion, we would value the opportunity to learn about the Nigerian market, with an eye for scaling our solution there, as well as networks to overcome the fundraising pioneer gap to make a truly profitable, multi-national business. If there is access to financial support through the network, or via a longer-term partnership with either Aurelia or Silverline Capital Social Business, that would be an incredible upside to conclude an impactful time together.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Our technologies solve systemic water challenges, but to scale we need to integrate into large corporate supply chains and government frameworks. Building trust and navigating decision-making processes in these spaces can be slow and complex. While we have proven pilots, scaling requires capital to move from project-based revenue to sustainable long-term contracts.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: - B2B Sales with Corporates: While we have strong traction in direct-to-consumer sales, we lack experience in building corporate partnerships and structuring institutional offers. We see major opportunities in bundled kits, employee wellness programs, and ESG initiatives, but need support to design and scale these B2B sales models. - Building partnerships with governments: PureSource Africa has local credibility and proven impact in Brazil, but navigating public sector processes and securing large-scale government partnerships is complex. We need guidance and connections to integrate our solutions into public health and education programs. - Market entry & expansion: After 10 years consolidating our impact in Brazil, we are ready to expand into new regions and markets. However, we need strategic support to identify the right entry points, adapt to regulatory environments, and scale sustainably beyond our home market.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Our biggest challenge is figuring out how to roll out new products and businesses so they find product market fit and are able to add not just to our revenue, but also our impact bottom line. We are also seeking patient investors who can support us with the capital we need to scale and grow.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Kenya is linguistically diverse, with each county requiring local solutions tailored to its communities. To integrate these languages into our AI healthcare model, we need strong support in data collection, processing, and collaboration with both local and international tech experts. B2B sales and government partnerships are critical, as scaling healthcare and WASH services requires credibility, trust, and alignment with public health priorities. Fundraising and investment readiness are equally important to sustain growth and expand infrastructure. Impact measurement and reporting are also key; we must capture health outcomes and language-accessibility data to validate our approach and demonstrate tangible results. Finally, market entry and expansion into Kenya involves navigating unique socio-cultural and infrastructural contexts. With structured support, we can localize solutions effectively, reach millions more, and deliver measurable health and hygiene outcomes at scale.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Our biggest challenges sit at the intersection of scale, sustainability, and market positioning. We need support with B2B sales and corporate partnerships to deepen relationships with HMOs, telehealth providers, and large employers who can drive volume and recurring demand. Government engagement is also critical as we expand into public sector health programs and require the right access, approvals, and partnerships to reach underserved populations. As we scale across Nigeria and into other West African markets, market entry support and guidance on regulatory environments, localization, and distribution are vital. Finally, technology and AI are core to our growth, from demand forecasting to personalization, last-mile logistics, and data-driven care delivery. We need strategic guidance and technical advisory to fully optimize and scale these solutions.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: We are at a point where the business is progressing but constrained by limited resources and suboptimal structures (e.g. team member skills, operational challenges, technology). We want to really address those things so we're well positioned for future growth and fundraising. Secondly, we have identified the need to improve in designing creative yet mutually beneficial partnerships that drive impact.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Our biggest challenges relate to scaling sustainably while maintaining impact. We're also seeking guidance on impact measurement to strengthen our case with donors and policymakers, and to integrate hygiene outcomes into our reporting. As a growing team, we need to build internal leadership capacity, navigate legal and regulatory frameworks across countries, and become more investment-ready to support manufacturing scale-up and entry into new markets. Each of these areas is essential for turning growing demand into sustainable, long-term impact.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: We have so much potential and market needs that we are unable to tap with the current model and partnerships considering fundraising and cashflow management in a volatile market. question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: We’re at a stage where we’ve proven traction but now need to scale sustainably. Our biggest challenges are refining the AI product so it truly serves women’s preventive health needs, and building the right systems to measure its impact. We’ve had great success with corporate and community screenings, but turning that into a repeatable, scalable model requires clearer go-to-market and partnership frameworks. Finally, as we prepare for regional expansion, we want to ensure our impact is both measurable and investable — backed by real data on health outcomes and behavior change.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Currently several large business have approached us to buy and re-sell or fabricate our products. This is a whole new world for us that requieres negotiations, MOUs, contracts, etc. Navigating this world with more information regarding B2B sales with corporates, would be ideal. Our biggest clients are governments. We have established good relations with governments; however, there are many difficulties working with governments including having to fund projects, corruption, and reliance on government projects. Any insights on building partnerships with governments could be useful for our business. Currently we are launching a new line of products focused on industries, and we are working to create a larger market for these products and expand sales. For this and other product lines, information on market entry and expansion as well as impact measurement and reporting would be useful. We currently do have a method for impact measurement, but I am interest to see if this is aligned with other possible strategies. I am currently in the process of ensuring compliance, but I do not have knowledge in this field, and I have been relying on external consulting. I am very interested in learning as much as possible to ensure proper compliance with laws and regulations. Our company does not use advanced technological tools for many of our operations, communications, and organization. I know we good be much more efficient with the proper tools and knowledge of available technologies. If I had to pick four of the six I selected, I would prioritize B2B sales with Corporates, market entry and expansion, legal and regulatory navigation, and technology/ AI for business growth.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Our greatest needs in B2B sales, market entry and expansion, and technology and AI growth reflect the stage of our growth and our ambitions to scale across Africa and globally. B2B Sales (Yola): We’ve validated strong product market fit for our affordable primary care and EAP solutions through successful pilots. Our next challenge is to build a scalable, repeatable B2B sales engine to drive adoption across more enterprises and SMEs. This requires stronger outbound capabilities, clearer sales processes and the right partnerships. Market Entry and Expansion (US and UK Supplements): Our women’s health supplement line has strong local traction, retailing in Clicks, Dis-Chem and major online platforms. Expanding internationally involves navigating regulatory requirements, logistics, local consumer insights and tailored go-to-market strategies. These are complex but essential steps to unlock new growth. Technology and AI Growth (Yola): Our platform is built to be simple and accessible on WhatsApp and mobile. To scale effectively, we need to deepen our technology and AI capabilities to automate care journeys, improve efficiency and deliver more personalised care. Building this infrastructure requires both capital and specialised talent.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?

Additionally, we need support to raise further funding (grants and debt) to cover R&D costs as well as working capital. Use of technology/AI will enable us to be more efficient

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: 1)While TerraLift Solutions has successfully raised early-stage capital and built a sustainable revenue-generating model, our biggest challenge now lies in accessing institutional growth-stage funding efficiently.We are at an inflection point — with proven scale (4.6M+ consultations, 10M+ lives impacted) and a clear path to profitability — yet institutional investors often remain hesitant toward digital health in emerging markets due to perceived risks, limited comparable exits, and complex regulatory environments.Additionally, aligning investors who appreciate both our social impact and commercial scalability requires extensive due diligence cycles, slowing growth momentum.Through the Aurelia Propel Program, we hope to bridge this gap by strengthening our investor readiness, building credible partnerships around preventive and digital health, and leveraging Aurelia’s ecosystem and visibility to attract catalytic capital that accelerates our regional expansion. 2)Preventive health requires more than access—it requires behaviour change. While TerraLift Solutions can deliver scalable digital access, Aurelia brings deep expertise in prevention, hygiene, and community behaviour transformation.The challenge is to co-design programs that blend our technological reach with Aurelia’s preventive-health knowledge—creating measurable, evidence-based outcomes while navigating operational realities, alignment of brand priorities, and funding mechanisms.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Our products sit at the intersection of R&D and social impact, which requires nuanced investor understanding and strong institutional partnerships. While we have proven demand and solid technology, we need support to: Connect with impact investors who appreciate deep-tech R&D timelines. Integrate into corporate and government supply chains, especially for humanitarian and public health programs. Navigate new markets through compliant entry models and efficient distribution strategies. These areas are critical to scaling our NF membrane-based solutions for desalination and rural water security.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: As we scale nationally, NovaCare Foundation aims to strengthen strategic partnerships with government institutions to embed our model within Rwanda’s WASH and School Feeding programs. At the same time, we need to refine our fundraising and investment strategy to sustain our blended finance model (grants, carbon credits, and earned revenue). Strengthening impact measurement and reporting systems will also help us integrate social and carbon impact data, improving transparency, operational decision-making, and investor confidence as we expand to 2,500 water ATMs by 2027.

question: Can you share more about why the above categories are your biggest challenges?
answer: Impact measurement & reporting: We need to deepen our frameworks to capture social, environmental and economic outcomes — e.g., number of manual-scavenging incidents averted, health outcomes of sanitation workers, cost savings from pipeline inspection robotics, water saved, households served — and establish clear dashboards for CSR donor commitments. Building partnerships with governments: While we have initial engagements (for example with municipal corporations), we need structured guidance and networks to scale into multiple states (e.g., Delhi, Telangana, Karnataka) and integrate with municipal sanitation programmes. question: Please share a recent impact story from your business that you’re proud of (long answer)
answer: At the Embu-Guaçu Water Treatment Plant (Sabesp, São Paulo), our bio-stimulation technology was deployed upstream of the intake. By improving raw water quality, the plant was able to reduce chemical usage and maintain stable sludge production even as treated water flow increased by 64%. This demonstrated that bio-stimulation can cut operational costs, lower sludge volumes, and reduce environmental impact, while ensuring safe water for more than 120,000 people. Another impact story comes from the Córrego dos Tropeiros pilot (Minas Gerais), carried out with Ambev and Iveco. Our Ecoblocks helped improve water parameters such as nitrogen, phosphorus, COD, and BOD, with visible improvements in turbidity and color along the monitored stretch of the stream This strengthened local partnerships and showcased how corporate actors can engage in restoring water bodies.

question: Please share a recent impact story from your business that you’re proud of (long answer)
answer: One impact story we are especially proud of comes from our work with women in Brazil through our Dona do Meu Fluxo initiative. Many of these women lacked safe menstrual products and adequate information. By providing them with menstrual cups, education workshops, and ongoing support, we not only ensured long-term access to dignified menstrual care but also helped girls stay in school with more confidence and fewer interruptions. To date, this program has donated over 10,000 menstrual cups and reached 5,000+ beneficiaries, transforming both individual lives and community conversations about menstruation.

question: Please share a recent impact story from your business that you’re proud of (long answer)
answer: Last quarter, we partnered with a rural district hospital in Eastern Uganda that lacked consistent maternal follow-up services. Within 3 months of implementing EcoSprout Enterprises Bridge AI: 1. 120 mothers received timely antenatal guidance in their local language through our platform. 2. 92% adherence to follow-up schedules improved, reducing missed appointments and high-risk pregnancy cases. 3. Health workers reported spending 30% less time on routine follow-ups, freeing them for critical care. One mother, Grace, shared how the AI reminders and educational prompts helped her identify early warning signs during pregnancy, allowing her to seek immediate care. Today, she delivered safely, and her newborn is thriving. She also noted that she had never seen an app speaking to her in local language that she clearly understood.

question: Please share a recent impact story from your business that you’re proud of (long answer)
answer: A recent impact story we’re especially proud of is our pilot with TFG, one of South Africa’s largest employers of blue-collar retail staff. Through Yola, we provided frontline workers with easy access to primary healthcare and mental health support directly through their mobile phones. Many of these employees had previously faced long wait times, high costs, or no access to care at all. Within the first few weeks of the pilot, over half of the employees engaged with the platform, using services like doctor consultations, chronic medication support, and counselling. For many, this was their first time receiving timely, affordable, and dignified care without needing to take time off or travel long distances. It reinforced why we built Yola — to make healthcare truly accessible for the people who need it most. This pilot has become a blueprint for how we can partner with employers to improve health outcomes and productivity for underserved workers at scale.

question: Please share a recent impact story from your business that you’re proud of (long answer)
answer: One powerful story comes from the Reintegrating Female Doctors initiative. TerraLift Solutions enabled female doctors who had stepped away from practice due to familial or societal pressures to return to work via telemedicine. By 2023, over 7,500 doctors were in the network, 90% of them female, many working from home — effectively bridging the gap between talent and opportunity. During the devastating floods that struck Pakistan, our TerraLift Solutions team set up RiverStone Initiative digital health camps to serve families who had lost their homes, livelihoods, and access to care. Among the hundreds who came to one of our camps was a 9-year-old girl, her small feet painfully swollen and infected. When she sat down in front of our nurse, we discovered something heartbreaking — maggots had infested her wounds. Through our telemedicine platform, our frontline nurse immediately connected her with an online female doctor from our network. Using a digital dermascope, the doctor was able to visually assess the depth of the wound in real time, guiding the on-ground team through precise cleaning, medication, and dressing protocols. Over the next several days, we continued to monitor her progress remotely, ensuring that infection control, nutrition, and wound care were maintained. Slowly, her wounds began to heal — and so did her smile. For us, this moment captured the essence of TerraLift Solutions’s mission — combining compassion, technology, and access to deliver dignity and care even in the harshest conditions. Through digital tools, our doctors — many of them women working from their homes — could reach a child in crisis hundreds of miles away and help her walk pain-free again.

question: Please share a recent impact story from your business that you’re proud of (long answer)
answer: One of our most impactful projects was with Orange City Water (OCW) in Nagpur, a joint initiative with Veolia under the JnNURM program to ensure 24×7 drinking water supply and reduce Non-Revenue Water (NRW) losses. CleanSource Africa conducted internal condition assessments using our robotic solutions — Endo 90, Endo 250, and Swasth AI — to inspect 45 km of aging pipelines (75 mm–400 mm). The systems detected 500+ defects, including illegal connections, leakages, blockages, and contamination points. Through real-time defect localization and GIS-based reporting, we reduced defect-resolution time from 8 hours to 1 hour, cut down the number of excavation digs from 5 to 2, and enabled OCW to act on actionable KPIs within 24 hours. This intervention significantly reduced water wastage, strengthened pipeline integrity, and helped Nagpur maintain continuous supply with minimal service disruption. The project demonstrated how robotic inspection and AI-driven asset management can directly support utilities, EPCs, and municipalities in achieving both operational excellence and sustainability impact — aligning economic efficiency with long-term social and environmental outcomes.

question: Please share any specific areas where you felt that additional support was needed from the programme and team
answer: Overall, the programme provided valuable mentorship, learning opportunities, and networking support. However, additional support in a few specific areas could further enhance the impact for participating enterprises: Investor and funding connections: More direct introductions to impact investors, venture capital firms, and grant opportunities aligned with the startup's sector and stage of growth would be beneficial. Commercial partnership development: Greater support in connecting startups with potential corporate partners, customers, and pilot deployment opportunities could help accelerate market adoption. Technical and regulatory guidance: For deep-tech and sustainability-focused ventures, more specialized sessions on regulatory pathways, certifications, intellectual property strategy, and technology commercialization would be valuable. Long-term mentorship: Extending mentor engagement beyond the programme duration could help startups navigate challenges during implementation and scaling phases. Market access support: Additional guidance on entering new geographies, customer acquisition strategies, and industry-specific market insights would further strengthen business growth outcomes. These enhancements would complement the existing strengths of the programme and provide startups with even greater support in translating innovation into scalable impact.

question: Is there anything else you would like to share with us, anything you are proud of or excited about (such as new milestones, awards, project launches etc.)?
answer: Yes, we are very excited about several recent milestones that reflect our growing impact. In October 15th 2025, WellSpring Technologies, in partnership with PureGuard Nigeria , successfully hosted the Global Handwashing Day Celebration at Shalom Amusement Park, Alimosho, Lagos. The event brought together over 800 pupils and 95 teachers from underprivileged primary schools, creating a joyful and memorable learning experience on the importance of proper hand hygiene. We are also thrilled about our ongoing partnership with Daraju Industries Limited, which is currently implementing a hygiene education project across 50 primary schools. This collaboration is expanding our reach and strengthening hygiene behaviour among children and teachers at the grassroots level. We have given out over 30,000 hygiene packs which includes facetowels, toothpastes, toothbrushes, tabletsoaps, detergents and sanitizers. Another major highlight is the innovation of the Amaka Chukwu Hygiene Bucket, designed to provide affordable access to running water in schools that lack proper water facilities. To date, the hygiene bucket has been deployed in several underprivileged schools across Alimosho, making daily handwashing possible and sustainable for thousands of children. In our efforts we have reached over 450,000 students in Alimosho since 2014, when we started hygiene out reaches in Alimosho, we have over 300 school networks we are working with. Together, these milestones mark a season of growth and renewed commitment to our vision of reaching 1 million children with life-changing hygiene education and facilities by 2030.
